In flight, males show expansive white patches near the wing tips and on the outer tail corners.
description.adult_female: Similar camouflage to the male but lacks the large white patches on the wings and tail (these spots are buff or absent).
description.juvenile: Plumage is softer and fluffier with less defined vermiculations; looks like a pale version of the female.
description.in_flight: Silent, erratic, and moth-like flight. It often flies low over cattle or open ground, twisting and turning to catch moths.

behaviour: Strictly nocturnal/crepuscular. During the day, it roosts on the ground, relying on camouflage. It becomes active at dusk, often sitting on dirt roads where its eyes reflect red in headlights ('eye-shine'). Its call is diagnostic: a rhythmic 'chuk-chuk-chuk-chuk-r-r-r', sounding exactly like a stone skipping over ice.
food: Moths, beetles, crickets, and winged termites caught on the wing.
did_you_know: Its generic name *Caprimulgus* means 'Goatsucker', based on an ancient myth that they suck milk from goats at night: in reality, they just hunt insects attracted to livestock.

habitat: Gardens, farmlands, forest edges, plantations, villages and open countryside; common around human habitation.

description.adult_male: Slim, long-tailed dove with warm grey-brown upperparts and pinkish buff underparts. Most distinctive feature is the black hind-neck patch covered with neat white spots. Long tail has white corners and black subterminal band.
description.adult_female: Very similar to male; may show slightly duller pink tones but not reliably distinguishable.
description.juvenile: Duller brown overall with buff-edged feathers; hind-neck patch poorly formed or absent.
description.in_flight: Fast direct flight with sharp wingbeats; white corners of tail prominent as the tail fans.

behaviour: Usually seen singly, in pairs or loose groups. Feeds mostly on the ground in open areas, often near grain stores or farmland. Walks with deliberate steps. Frequently perches on wires and house roofs. Soft cooing call, a repeated, mellow 'coo-coo-coo'. Generally tame and well adapted to human landscapes.
food: Seeds and grains, small fruits, and occasional insects picked from the ground.

habitat: Open woodlands, grassy slopes with scattered acacia or scrub, and forest edges. Unlike the Olive-backed Pipit, it avoids deep shade and leaf litter, preferring open grassy patches with clear perches.

description.adult_male: A neat, streaky brown pipit. Upperparts are warm brown with heavy blackish streaking (lacking the greenish-olive tone of the Olive-backed Pipit). The breast is buff with bold black streaks that continue down the flanks as finer lines. **Key ID Feature:** It has a short, curved hindclaw (unlike the long, straight spur of the Paddyfield Pipit), though this is hard to see. The facial pattern is relatively plain with a weak supercilium.
description.adult_female: Identical to the male in plumage.
description.juvenile: Upperparts are more scaly; underparts are paler with less distinct streaking.
description.in_flight: Flight is buoyant and undulating. It gives a characteristic, harsh, buzzing call 'teez' or 'speek', which is quite different from the thin whistle of the Olive-backed Pipit.

behaviour: True to its name, it flushes into trees when disturbed, rather than flying far away over the ground like meadow pipits. It walks with a steady gait (not hopping). Before spring migration, males may be heard practicing their song from tree tops.
food: Small insects, weevils, beetles, and grass seeds.
did_you_know: During the breeding season (in the Himalayas/Europe), it performs a spectacular 'parachute' display flight, singing while descending with wings held up in a V-shape.

habitat: Shade-loving. Found in the understorey of evergreen forests, sholas, coffee plantations, and damp wooded ravines. It loves leaf litter and is rarely seen in open sunny grasslands.

description.adult_male: Distinctly greenish-olive above (not warm brown like the Tree Pipit) with faint streaking on the back. **Key ID Features:** 1) A bold supercilium (eyebrow) that is buff in front of the eye and white behind it. 2) A diagnostic black-and-white spot pattern on the ear coverts (often appearing as two pale spots separated by a dark line). Underparts are whitish with bold black streaks on the breast and flanks.
description.adult_female: Identical to the male in plumage.
description.juvenile: Browner above with more spotting; less green.
description.in_flight: Flushes silently or with a thin, high-pitched 'tseep' into the mid-canopy of nearby trees. It rarely flies long distances when disturbed.

behaviour: Highly arboreal for a pipit. When flushed, it flies up into a tree and walks along horizontal branches, pumping its tail slowly up and down. On the ground, it is incredibly camouflaged among dead leaves, often allowing a very close approach before exploding upwards.
food: Insects, spiders, and seeds found in forest leaf litter.

photos.caption: Olive-backed Pipit foraging on moss-covered rocks.

did_you_know: It is famously difficult to spot until it moves; its olive-green back matches the shade of the forest floor perfectly.

habitat: Moist deciduous and evergreen forests, well-wooded slopes, teak plantations and mixed forest from lowlands to ~1800 m.

description.adult_male: Compact, dark woodshrike with bold dark mask through eye, lacking pale supercilium. Grey-brown upperparts with darker wings and tail; white rump striking. Underparts whitish with grey wash on breast and flanks.
description.adult_female: Very similar to male, slightly duller. Mask marginally narrower.
description.juvenile: Duller and browner overall with reduced mask; upperparts show slight scaling.
description.in_flight: White rump very prominent; slow, bounding flight through mid-storey and canopy.

behaviour: Usually singly or in pairs, often perching upright high in canopy. Gleans insects from leaves and small branches, sometimes sallying short distances. Frequently joins mixed-species flocks in evergreen forest. Calls are harsh, nasal chattering notes.
food: Insects including caterpillars, beetles and other arthropods gleaned from foliage.

habitat: High-altitude montane grasslands above ~1100 m, especially wet meadows, marshy depressions, edges of shola patches, reed beds and lush, tall grass on slopes. Strongly associated with saturated grasslands in the southern and central Western Ghats.

description.adult_male: Warm brown upperparts with fine streaking on mantle and a clear buff supercilium. Tail distinctly broad, long, rounded and graduated—with fine dark barring above and dark underside contrasting with pale/white tips. Underparts whitish with buff wash on breast and flanks. Gape black, especially noticeable during singing.
description.adult_female: Structurally identical to male. Plumage similar but gape brown rather than black, and overall tones slightly duller.
description.juvenile: Duller brown with less contrast in tail pattern; buff tones weaker on supercilium and flanks. Underparts more evenly washed buff.
description.in_flight: Male performs characteristic song-flights: fluttering vertical ascent, tail widely fanned to display the broad rounded feathers, combined with a trilling, buzzing call before dropping back suddenly into grass cover. Non-display flight is low and brief.

behaviour: Outside breeding season, extremely skulking—moves mouse-like through dense, wet grass, seldom rising above cover. During breeding, becomes vocal and visible: males ascend in fluttering song-flights with lark-like buzzing trills. Gives sharp 'chack', thin 'tzeep' and metallic 'zink' notes from within grass clumps. Highly site-faithful to specific wet meadows. Distribution patchy and localised despite being resident across suitable high-altitude grasslands.
food: Small insects and arthropods gleaned from grass stems, leaves and ground-level vegetation.

photos.caption: Broad-tailed Grassbird in wet montane grassland, Brahmagiri Wildlife Sanctuary, Karnataka.

did_you_know: Its unusually broad, rounded tail is unique among Asian grassbirds and is displayed dramatically during buzzing song-flights.

habitat: Lowland riparian forests, riverine belts, and moist deciduous forests at the foothills. Unlike the Great Hornbill, it avoids high-altitude rainforests, preferring areas near waterbodies (e.g., Dandeli, Konkan streams).

description.adult_male: A large, pied hornbill. Plumage is glossy black with a white belly, white throat, and white trailing edge to the wings. The massive yellow-and-black casque is shaped like a high, single-bladed axe ('axe-blade' shape), with a large black patch on the front. **Key ID Feature:** The outer tail feathers are pure white (not black-tipped), while the central pair is black. The bare skin around the eye is blue-black.
description.adult_female: Similar to the male but smaller. **Diagnostic Feature:** The bare skin around the eye is white or pinkish-flesh coloured (unlike the black skin of the male). The bill and casque are slightly smaller.
description.juvenile: Bill is small and pale yellow; the casque is undeveloped or just a small ridge. The eye patch is pale.
description.in_flight: Flight is labored and heavy, with a few flaps followed by a glide. The all-white outer tail feathers and the broad white trailing edge of the wings create a striking pattern. The noise of the wings is loud but lacks the heavy 'chugging' sound of the Great Hornbill.

behaviour: Noisy and gregarious. Usually found in pairs or small noisy flocks that congregate at fruiting trees (especially Ficus and Strychnos). Their call is a raucous, shrill, cackling 'kak-kak-kak-kak' that rises in pitch. They are famously fond of riverine habitats and often fly across water channels in the evening to roost.
food: Predominantly frugivorous (figs, drupes); also takes small fish (from drying pools), lizards, and large insects.

photos.caption: Dustbathing times...

did_you_know: It is popularly known as the 'River Hornbill' due to its strong association with watercourses and riparian vegetation. The Malabar Pied Hornbill also performs a signature 'dust bath' to get rid of parasites and excess oil from its feathers.
